ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general pasta dish class (FOODON_00002473); no specific arrabbiata entry exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers cooked pasta as a base; no specific arrabbiata sauce variant exists in FoodEx2. Closest parent match used for the pasta component.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for pasta all'arrabbiata as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of pasta all'arrabbiata is approximately 350 g, comprising boiled pasta tossed in spicy tomato sauce, typically plated as a main course.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Pasta, cooked, enriched (FDC ID 168936) and Tomato sauce, canned (FDC ID 170457), SR Legacy
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA), Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA FoodData Central entry for pasta all'arrabbiata exists. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked enriched pasta (FDC ID 168936) and canned tomato sauce (FDC ID 170457), combined in approximate proportions consistent with a standard arrabbiata recipe (roughly 60% pasta, 35% tomato sauce, 5% olive oil and aromatics). Input values from the source record (energy 120 kcal, protein 3.75 g, fat 4.5 g, carbohydrate 16.25 g, sodium 187.5 mg per 100 g) are broadly consistent with this USDA-derived composite and have been retained with medium confidence. Cholesterol is confirmed 0 mg as the dish contains no animal products. Trans fat is confirmed 0 g by formulation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. American Journal of Clinical Nutrition. 2021;114(5):1625-1632.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for pasta all'arrabbiata exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 45 is estimated from the closest analogous approved-source entry: white pasta, boiled al dente (GI range 38–52, central estimate 45), as reported in Atkinson FS et al. (2021), American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 114(5):1625-1632. The tomato-based sauce and olive oil content may further lower the effective GI through fat and acid effects on gastric emptying. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (45) × available carbohydrate per serving (16.25 g/100 g × 350 g = 56.875 g) ÷ 100 = 25.6. GL is high (>20) at a full restaurant portion.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 187.5 mg/100 g; potassium ~280 mg/100 g from tomatoes and vegetables
hypertension.guidance: Sodium content is below 200 mg/100 g and the potassium-rich tomato base supports DASH dietary principles; however, at a 350 g restaurant portion total sodium reaches approximately 656 mg, so individuals with hypertension should monitor portion size and request low-sodium canned tomatoes.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined pasta GI ~45 (low individually) but glycemic load per full serving is high (~26); 16.25 g carbohydrate/100 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although the GI of white pasta is low to medium, a standard 350 g restaurant serving delivers approximately 57 g of available carbohydrate with a glycemic load of ~26, which is clinically significant for type 2 diabetes management; individuals should request wholegrain or legume-based pasta, reduce portion size, and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Olive oil monounsaturated fat, lycopene from tomatoes, zero cholesterol, low saturated fat 0.75 g/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The olive oil base provides cardioprotective monounsaturated fatty acids (~2.8 g/100 g) and the tomato sauce delivers lycopene, a carotenoid associated with reduced LDL oxidation; saturated fat is low at 0.75 g/100 g and cholesterol is zero, making this dish broadly compatible with a heart-healthy dietary pattern when portion size is controlled.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade vegetables (tomato, chilli) contain solanine alkaloids that may aggravate joint inflammation in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Tomatoes and chilli are nightshade vegetables; RA-sensitive individuals should monitor symptom response carefully, and the anti-inflammatory polyphenols from olive oil, garlic, and fresh herbs may partially offset this concern for tolerant individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pasta all'Arrabbiata is a classic Italian pasta dish originating from the Lazio region of Italy, whose name translates literally as 'angry pasta' in reference to the fiery heat of the red chilli. The dish is prepared by boiling pasta — traditionally penne rigate — until al dente, then tossing it in a sauce of sautéed garlic, crushed red chilli, and ripe tomatoes cooked in extra-virgin olive oil, finished with fresh flat-leaf parsley. The result is a bold, tangy, and spicy sauce with a silky olive-oil emulsion coating each piece of pasta. The dish is naturally vegan and dairy-free, containing no meat, cheese, or cream. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides approximately 120 kcal, 3.75 g protein, 4.5 g fat (predominantly heart-healthy monounsaturated fat from olive oil), and 16.25 g carbohydrate with 2.0 g dietary fibre. Sodium is moderate at 187.5 mg/100 g. The tomato base contributes lycopene, vitamin C (~10 mg/100 g), and potassium (~280 mg/100 g), supporting cardiovascular and endothelial health. The glycemic index of the pasta component is approximately 45 (low), though the glycemic load at a full restaurant serving of 350 g is high (~26), making portion control important for individuals managing type 2 diabetes. The dish is widely served in Italian restaurants across Singapore and Southeast Asia, often adapted with additional vegetables such as zucchini, bell peppers, or carrots. It is suitable for vegetarian and vegan diets and is free from dairy and cholesterol.
common_user_queries: How many calories in pasta arrabbiata?
Is pasta arrabbiata good for diabetics?
What is the GI of pasta arrabbiata?
Is arrabbiata pasta high in sodium?
Can I eat pasta arrabbiata if I have hypertension?
What pasta is used in arrabbiata?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for pasta all'arrabbiata exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore, USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite entries for cooked enriched pasta (FDC ID 168936) and canned tomato sauce (FDC ID 170457), and are broadly consistent with the input source record. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database via Atkinson et al. (2021). Medium confidence is appropriate given the adapted source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked enriched pasta (FDC ID 168936) and canned tomato sauce (FDC ID 170457); no exact arrabbiata entry exists in any approved source. Input source values retained as they are consistent with the USDA-derived composite. 2. Cholesterol is confirmed 0 mg: the dish contains no animal products (no meat, dairy, or eggs). Trans fat is confirmed 0 g by product formulation (no hydrogenated fats used). 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific prepared arrabbiata dish are not individually reported in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or the Singapore HPB database as of 2026-04-16; the composite preparation method prevents reliable disaggregation of these values from available approved sources. 4. Potassium estimated at ~280 mg/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central values for tomato sauce and pasta components; this is an approximation. 5. Monounsaturated fat (~2.8 g/100 g) and polyunsaturated fat (~0.6 g/100 g) estimated from USDA olive oil and pasta fat profiles; not directly reported in input. 6. Two portions were listed in the input with conflicting is_default flags (400 g and 350 g both marked true); 350 g has been adopted as the default serving size as the more conservative and commonly cited restaurant pasta portion. 7. This dish is served in Singapore as an Italian restaurant item; it is not a Singaporean dish and therefore the Singaporean multilingual naming requirements (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) do not apply. The cuisine is Italian and no mandatory local-language names are required under the multilingual matrix for Italian cuisine. 8. GI glycemic load per serving calculated at 350 g serving: (45 × 56.875) ÷ 100 = 25.6.

q1_food_identity.notes: Canonical English name 'Pasta all'Arrabbiata' is precise and unambiguous. Italian cuisine does not require mandatory local-language names under the multilingual matrix; G6 is satisfied.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal, protein_g, fat_g, carbohydrate_g all present and numeric. Nutrient source is USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (approved primary source), adapted match documented in source_match_notes.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (2.0), sodium_mg (187.5), saturated_fat_g (0.75), sugar_g (2.5) all present as numeric values and plausible.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (45), gi_category ('low'), glycemic_load_per_serving (25.6), and gi_source all present. GI source is University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. (2021) American Journal of Clinical Nutrition — approved GI source. Analogous estimate basis documented.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn FOODON_00002473 (pasta dish) used as closest_parent match. FoodEx2 A00LC (Pasta, cooked) used as closest_parent match. SNOMED CT null with gap note. All match_type values conform to allowed values: closest_parent, exact, none.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters, covers preparation method, taste/texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes (machine key type2_diabetes, human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes'), Cardiovascular Disease. Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: Flat aliases array contains all values from aliases_by_language. English aliases are comprehensive covering original input name, common variants, and romanized forms. Italian cuisine does not require mandatory non-English aliases under the multilingual matrix.
